---
title: "S2: Sphere Scaffolding"
---

S2 pages are scaffolded as the Living Book grows beyond the S1 interactive core.

S2 is the first step where the book asks how circles can be organized into a sphere-like layer. The safest current version is finite and combinatorial: poles, equators, latitude-like rows, longitude-like addresses, and antipodal pairings are tracked as explicit data rather than assumed from continuous geometry.

::: {.learning-unit}

## What To Learn Here

Read S2 as a bridge, not as a finished theory of smooth spheres. The goal is to see how S1 circular addresses can be suspended into a finite sphere scaffold while keeping every claim tied to checked finite definitions.

:::

<div class="warning-box">
This section distinguishes Lean-proved finite suspended-circle facts from future continuous sphere geometry and future interactives. The theorem cards are the status source.
</div>

## Lesson Path

1. Start with [Suspended Circles](suspended_circles.qmd) to see how a finite circle gets two pole-like endpoints.
2. Continue to [Sphere Grids](sphere_grids.qmd) for latitude/longitude vocabulary and finite grid cautions.
3. Return to the theorem cards when you need to know which finite claims are Lean-proved.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Explain the difference between a finite suspended-circle model and a claim about the continuous sphere `S^2`.

:::

## Source Trail

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="S2-T0001"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="S2-0001"></div>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
